Let's answer that.</reasoning>
In the official rules of Dodgeball (the type of game you play in schools, clubs, and at

the world‑sized tournaments), there isn’t a formal “5 D” list.  However, many coaching programs have adopted a handy **5 Ds of Dodgeball** as a quick‑reference way to describe the core elements of a good defensive strategy.  
The most common version

of this list is:

| **D** | Description (why it matters) |
|-------|------------------------------|
| **Dodge** |  The most basic survival skill – move your body to avoid the ball. This is the single most important thing a player can do. |
| **Distance** |  Keep a healthy gap between you and the ball’s trajectory. A well‑maintained distance gives you more time to react, better view of teammates

, and a safer zone for your team. |
| **Defense** |  Use hands, shoulders, elbows, or even a bat/ball‑tying stick to block or deflect incoming balls. A good defensive posture supports teammates who might otherwise get hit. |
| **Delay** |  Between the ball’s impact and where it lands, you can momentarily “stall” the action –

for instance, by darting forward or sidestepping – which can give your teammates seconds to reposition or escape. |
| **Discard** (or “Drop”) |  When you catch a ball you need to **drop** it safely into your hand, keeping it in play so you stay alive. Coaches emphasize the “drop‑into‑hand” technique that prevents accidental

throws and guarantees a clean catch. |

**Why these five?**  
All of the first‑hand defensive play in dodgeball circles back to the same essentials: avoid, space, block, buy time, and bring yourself into a safe state.  The five Ds give a concise coaching phrase that coaches use, for example, in

drills, warm‑up talks, and quick‑tactics during a game.

---

> **Side note:**  
> Some coaching circles also talk about the “FIVE C’s” of dodgeball (Catch, Counter‑throw, Combatives, Awareness, and Composure), or the “FIVE T’s” of the offensive side (Throw, Time
, Teamwork, Tactics, and Tenacity).  The “5 Ds” are the most widely cited defensive shorthand, but you’ll find variations depending on the coach or program.
